preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiced labio-velar approximant /w/ to the voiceless glottal fricative /h/ is a documented sound change, often occurring through processes of debuccalization or lenition. This shift involves the loss of the labial and velar articulation, resulting in a glottal fricative, frequently accompanied by devoicing.
phoneticEffects: Debuccalization, lenition, devoicing, aspiration, loss of labial and velar articulation, shift to glottal place of articulation

examples.note: Shift in specific environments (the 'ἑορτή rule') involving devoicing and aspiration.
Syllable-final /w/ becomes /h/ in Cuisnahuat dialect, as seen in the past participle of 'to buy'.
Syllable-final /w/ becomes /h/ in Cuisnahuat dialect, as seen in the past participle of 'to count'.
A phonological rule changes /w/ to /h/ when it occurs word-finally after a rounded vowel.
Word-final /w/ frequently changes to /h/ in many dialects.

sources: Sturm, J. (2017). w > h in Attic Greek. Academia.edu.
Campbell, L. (1985). The Pipil Language of El Salvador. Mouton de Gruyter.
Larsen, T. W. (1988). Manifestations of Ergativity in Quiché Grammar. University of California, Berkeley.
Campbell, L., & Langacker, R. W. (1978). Proto-Nahua and Proto-Uto-Aztecan. International Journal of American Linguistics, 44(2), 107-132.
